**Background:** Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) is a life-threatening hyperinflammatory syndrome characterized by uncontrolled immune activation, excessive cytokine release, and multi-organ damage. In adults, HLH is most often secondary to infections, malignancies, and autoimmune conditions, yet remains underrecognized with high mortality. The expanding use of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy and immune checkpoint inhibitors, together with COVID-19-associated hyperinflammation, has renewed interest in HLH pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management. This systematic review synthesizes the adult HLH literature from 2016 to 2026 to map the evidence landscape and identify research gaps.

**Methods:** A systematic search was conducted across Scopus, PubMed, and Embase using predefined terms related to hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis, macrophage activation syndrome, cytokine storm, and hyperinflammation in adults. Records were screened following PRISMA guidelines. A journal quality threshold of CiteScore 3.0 or higher was applied to ensure inclusion of high-impact evidence. Data extraction focused on etiology, pathogenesis, diagnostic criteria, and therapeutic approaches.

**Results:** The search identified 897 records, of which 783 remained after deduplication. Following screening, 50 articles across 36 journals met inclusion criteria, covering the period 2016 to 2021 with an average of 945 citations per article. Key thematic areas included the role of cytokine cascades (interleukin-1, interleukin-6, interleukin-18, and interferon-gamma) in disease pathogenesis; refinement of diagnostic tools including the HLH-2004 criteria, HScore, and macrophage activation syndrome classification criteria; evolving etiological triggers such as CAR T-cell-associated cytokine release syndrome and COVID-19-related hyperinflammation; and emerging therapies including emapalumab, ruxolitinib, and targeted cytokine blockade.

**Conclusions:** The adult HLH literature reflects a rapidly evolving field driven by advances in immunotherapy and the impact of COVID-19. Diagnostic criteria remain imperfect for adult populations, and prospective trials are urgently needed. Future research should prioritize adult-specific diagnostic algorithms, biomarker-guided treatment protocols, and standardized outcome measures to improve early recognition and survival.
